601: First emperor of the Holy Roman Empire, he revived the imperial idea in the West.
602: Duke of Normandy who conquered England after the Battle of Hastings in 1066.
603: First king of the Plantagenet dynasty and husband of Eleanor of Aquitaine.
604: King of France who consolidated royal power and participated in the Third Crusade.
605: King of France and saint who led both the Seventh and Eighth Crusades.
606: King of France who suppressed the Knights Templar and moved the papacy to Avignon.
607: King of England who conquered Wales and fought against Scotland.
608: King of England who launched the Hundred Years' War against France.
609: King of England, victorious at Agincourt during the Hundred Years' War.
610: Last Plantagenet king, who died at Bosworth ending the Wars of the Roses.
611: Founder of the Tudor dynasty, who defeated Richard III at Bosworth.
612: King of England who broke with Rome and founded the Church of England.
613: First wife of Henry VIII and daughter of the Catholic Monarchs, whose divorce triggered the Anglican schism.
614: Minister of Henry VIII and architect of the Anglican Reformation and the dissolution of the monasteries.
615: Catholic queen of England nicknamed 'Bloody Mary' for her persecution of Protestants.
616: First king of Asturias, who initiated the Reconquista after the Battle of Covadonga.
617: King of León and Castile who conquered Toledo in 1085 and was both patron and rival of El Cid.
618: King of Castile and León who conquered Córdoba and Seville during the Reconquista.
619: King of Castile and León who promoted Castilian as a language of culture and established the Toledo School of Translators.
620: King of Aragon who conquered Mallorca and Valencia for the Christian crown.
621: Last Nasrid king of Granada, who surrendered the city to the Catholic Monarchs in 1492.
622: Scholastic theologian and philosopher, known as the father of the ontological argument.
623: French philosopher and theologian, pioneer of the scholastic method and famous for his romance with Héloïse.
624: French philosopher and writer, celebrated for her love correspondence with Abelard.
625: German medieval abbess, mystic, composer, and polymath.
626: Cistercian monk who promoted the Second Crusade and reformed monastic life.
627: German Dominican philosopher and scientist, master of Thomas Aquinas.
628: Scottish Franciscan philosopher and theologian, known as the 'Subtle Doctor'.
629: Italian Franciscan theologian, Doctor of the Church and biographer of Saint Francis.
630: Dominican mystic and reformer, Doctor of the Church who was influential in returning the papacy to Rome.
631: Italian mathematician who introduced Arabic numerals to Europe and described the famous sequence that bears his name.
632: Grandson of Genghis Khan and founder of the Yuan dynasty in China.
633: Turco-Mongol conqueror and founder of the Timurid Empire with its capital at Samarkand.
634: Founder of the Mughal Empire in India, descendant of Tamerlane and Genghis Khan.
635: First emperor of a unified China, who ordered the construction of the Great Wall and the Terracotta Army.
636: Han emperor who expanded the Chinese empire and consolidated Confucianism as the official ideology.
637: The only empress to rule China in her own right, founder of the short-lived Zhou dynasty.
638: Second emperor of the Tang dynasty, considered one of China's greatest rulers.
639: Chinese admiral of the Ming dynasty who led seven major maritime expeditions as far as Africa.
640: Japanese regent who promoted Buddhism and organized the state according to the Chinese model.
641: Lady of the Heian court and author of 'The Tale of Genji', considered the world's first novel.
642: Japanese writer of the Heian court and author of 'The Pillow Book'.
643: First shogun of Japan and founder of the Kamakura shogunate.
644: Japanese daimyo who initiated the unification of Japan during the Sengoku period.
645: Japanese daimyo who completed the unification of Japan after the death of Oda Nobunaga.
646: Founder of the Tokugawa shogunate, which governed Japan for 250 years.
647: Florentine painter and pioneer of perspective in the Quattrocento.
648: Florentine goldsmith and sculptor, creator of the 'Gates of Paradise' for the Florence Baptistery.
649: Flemish painter, master of oil painting and creator of the Ghent Altarpiece.
650: German painter and engraver, the foremost figure of the Northern Renaissance.
651: German painter and court portraitist of Henry VIII of England.
652: Flemish painter and author of 'The Garden of Earthly Delights' and other symbolic visions.
653: Flemish painter renowned for his peasant scenes and landscapes.
654: Florentine Dominican painter and author of the frescoes at the Convent of San Marco.
655: Quattrocento painter and master of mathematical perspective.
656: Quattrocento painter celebrated for his mastery of perspective and foreshortening.
657: Venetian painter and central figure of the Venetian school of the Renaissance.
658: Venetian painter and forerunner of Titian's tonal style.
659: Italian Renaissance painter who anticipated the Baroque with his luministic effects.
660: Florentine Mannerist goldsmith and sculptor, creator of 'Perseus' and a celebrated autobiography.
661: Venetian architect and author of 'The Four Books of Architecture', a defining reference for classicism.
662: Italian architect and first master builder of the new Basilica of Saint Peter in Rome.
663: Italian painter and biographer, author of 'Lives of the Artists', the first history of Renaissance art.
664: Florentine philosopher, translator of Plato, and central figure of Renaissance Neoplatonism.
665: Italian humanist philosopher and author of the 'Oration on the Dignity of Man'.
666: Florentine Dominican friar and reformist preacher who briefly governed Florence before being executed.
667: Swiss physician and alchemist, father of toxicology and a revolutionary figure in Renaissance medicine.
668: Flemish anatomist and author of 'De humani corporis fabrica', founder of modern anatomy.
669: Flemish cartographer and creator of the map projection that bears his name.
670: Spanish conquistador, partner and rival of Pizarro in the conquest of Peru, who also explored Chile.
671: Spanish conquistador and founder of Santiago de Chile, killed by the Mapuche.
672: Spanish conquistador and the first European to navigate the Amazon River to its mouth.
673: Spanish conquistador who founded Bogotá and conquered the Muisca territory of New Granada.
674: Basque conquistador famous for his rebellion against the Spanish Crown during the expedition to El Dorado.
675: Spanish conquistador, lieutenant of Cortés and conqueror of Guatemala.
676: Spanish conquistador, the first European to explore Florida and governor of Puerto Rico.
677: Spanish conquistador who explored the southeastern United States and was the first European to discover the Mississippi River.
678: Spanish conquistador who led a disastrous expedition to Florida, from which Cabeza de Vaca was among the few survivors.
679: Basque navigator who completed the first circumnavigation of the globe after the death of Magellan.
680: Inca emperor who transformed Cusco into an imperial capital and greatly expanded the Tahuantinsuyo.
681: Inca emperor and son of Pachacútec who expanded the empire to its greatest extent.
682: Last great Inca emperor before the arrival of the Spanish, who died of smallpox.
683: Inca of Cusco, defeated in the civil war against his brother Atahualpa.
684: Inca leader who led the great rebellion against the Spanish from the fortress of Vilcabamba.
685: Fourth tlatoani of Tenochtitlan, founder of the Triple Alliance and true architect of the Aztec Empire.
686: Poet-tlatoani of Texcoco, member of the Triple Alliance and a central cultural figure of the Mexica world.
687: Eighth Mexica tlatoani who expanded the Aztec Empire to its greatest extent.
688: Tenth Mexica tlatoani who led the 'Noche Triste' against the Spanish and died of smallpox shortly after.
689: Last Mexica tlatoani who resisted the Spanish siege of Tenochtitlan and was executed by Hernán Cortés.
690: Maya ruler of Palenque, one of the most celebrated for his tomb and monuments.
691: Son and successor of Pakal at Palenque, who completed the temples of the Cross Group.
692: Founder of the Maya dynasty of Copán, in present-day Honduras.
693: Maya ruler of Copán and artistic patron, who was captured and executed by the king of Quiriguá.
694: Mapuche toqui who led the resistance against Spanish conquistadors in Arauco.
695: Mapuche toqui who led the resistance after the death of Lautaro against the Spanish in Chile.
696: Mythical founder of the Curacazgo of Cusco and legendary origin of the Inca dynasty.
697: K'iche' hero who died fighting Pedro de Alvarado during the conquest of Guatemala.
698: Taino chieftess of Hispaniola, executed by the Spanish following a massacre at Jaragua.
699: Taino chieftain who led the resistance against Spanish conquistadors in Cuba.
700: First viceroy of New Spain and later of Peru, organizer of early colonial government.
